    Foreword¶

    The Government of Uganda is committed to improving the quality of health services across the country through a combination of strategic interventions. Client-centered healthcare approaches have emerged as essential paradigms, emphasizing the active involvement, preferences, and experiences of clients in shaping health care delivery strategies.

    Integrating routine client feedback into health service delivery is key in ensuring that services are responsive to client expectations and needs. To achieve this, it’s important to engage stakeholders from all levels of our health system. This approach will help harmonize and standardize the process of obtaining client feedback across Uganda’s health sector.

    In collaboration with stakeholders, the Ministry of Health has developed a straightforward tool to facilitate routine client feedback. Accompanying guidelines have been developed to aid in the e昀昀ective implementation of this initiative and to leverage client feedback to meet the expectations of health service users. We extend our gratitude to the diverse stakeholders whose contributions were instrumental in developing the tool and guidelines. We are con昀椀dent that these resources will empower both public and private sector healthcare providers to enhance their services, thereby meeting the needs of the people we serve e昀昀ectively.

    Dr. Diana Atwine Permanent Secretary Ministry of Health, Uganda

    • Routine Client Feedback Satisfaction Implementation Guidelines iii

    Acknowledgement¶

    The development of the routine client satisfaction feedback tool and guidelines was made possible by Ministry of Health under the guidance of the Permanent Secretary, the Director General of Health Services, Director Governance and Regulations, and coordinated by the Department of Standards, Compliance, Accreditation and Patient Protection (SCAPP-D).

    We acknowledge the following partners: development partners (WHO, USAID, CDC), for their technical support; implementing partners USAID Uganda Health Activity (UHA) for the 昀椀nancial and technical support; Monitoring Evaluation Technical Support (METS) Program, Uganda Catholic Medical Bureau (UCMB), Uganda Protestant Medical Bureau (UPMB), USAID Social Behavior Change Activity (SBCA), Infectious Diseases Institute (IDI), for their technical support. We appreciate the health facilities and health workers that participated in the pilot.

    Dr. Henry G. Mwebesa Director General Health Services Ministry of Health, Uganda

    Recommended citation: Uganda Ministry of Health. Routine client feedback implementation guidelines. Kampala (Uganda): MOH; 2024

    • Routine Client Feedback Satisfaction Implementation Guidelines iv

    1.1 Introduction¶

    The Ministry of Health (MoH) has the responsibility to develop and implement national health policies, standards and guidelines required to operationalise Government health interventions.

    These guidelines are meant to outline the process of engaging clients as key stakeholders in the management of health services and to get feedback on the quality of health services provided in health facilities so that gaps are identified and addressed to improve the quality of services in the national health system. The routine client satisfaction feedback tool included in this guide contains key aspects of healthcare service delivery to ensure that emerging issues are addressed and services are responsive to their expectations/needs.

    1.2 Who should use this guide?¶

    The guide applies to different levels of stakeholders from the national to community level including, MoH departments, health facility managers (public and private), health care workers, District Health Teams (DHTs), community actors, and implementing partners.

    1.3 General objective¶

    To provide guidance on how to implement the routine client feedback initiative using standardized tools across the national health system

    1.3.1 Specific objectives of the guide¶
    • • To describe the organizational arrangements and actors that will be involved in the implementation of routine client feedback
    • • To systematically guide health workers on how to collect, analyze and use routine client feedback information to improve services
    • • To provide elaborate and harmonized routine client feedback mechanism across the health service delivery system
    • • To describe the appropriate tools to use in the routine client feedback initiative

    2.1 Implementation approach¶

    The implementation of the initiative involves the various levels at national, regional, district, health facility and community.

    2.1.1 At national level¶
    • • The MoH will be responsible for implementing this initiative through the Directorate of Governance and Regulation that will report directly to the Director General of Health Services and the Permanent Secretary.
    • • The MoH will develop, update and distribute the relevant tools for implementing the routine client feedback initiative at all levels.
    • • The MoH will mobilize resources and coordinate the implementation of the routine client feedback initiative.

    Routine Client Feedback Satisfaction Implementation Guidelines 1

    2.1.2 At sub-national level¶

    The Regional Referral Hospital (RRH)-Public Health Department (PHD) will take lead in planning and implementing the routine client feedback initiative in the RRHs and districts/urban authorities within their catchment areas.

    2.1.3 At district level¶

    The District Health Officer (DHO)/City Health Officers will coordinate the routine client feedback initiative for all health facilities in their respective districts/cities.

    2.1.4 At health facility level¶

    This is the primary implementing unit that includes: National Referral Hospitals (NRH), Regional Referral Hospitals (RRH), General Hospitals (GH), Health Centers IV, III and II. The health facility head will be responsible for implementing the routine client feedback initiative including taking immediate actions to improve quality of services.

    2.1.5 Community level¶

    The health facility in-charge will be responsible for implementing the routine client feedback initiative within the designated catchment area.

    2.2 Orientation¶

    The various stakeholders shall be oriented and guided on the process of implementing the routine client feedback initiative using virtual platforms and where applicable hybrid, as follows:

    • 2.2.1 National level

    MoH will orient the central team, National referrals, RRH leadership, Public Health Department (PHD) and implementing partners including the private sector etc.

    • 2.2.2 Regional level

    The RRH through the PHD, in collaboration with regional health partners, will plan, coordinate and orient key actors in the RRH, districts and urban authorities including the private sector.

    • 2.2.3 District level

    The districts in collaboration with health partners will orient district/urban/health facility teams.

    2.3.4 Health facility level

    The head of the health facility leadership shall designate a person to orient the facility teams and coordinate implementation of the routine client feedback initiative at the facility level.

    3.1 Procedure for obtaining routine client feedback¶

    This section describes the intervals for obtaining client feedback; who should collect client feedback, the procedure for obtaining client feedback, the timing and place of interview, the

    number of clients to be interviewed and how they should be distributed per level of health facility; client rights and exclusion criteria.

    3.1.1 Introducing the routine client feedback initiative to the clients during routine care¶

    The routine client feedback initiative shall be implemented routinely in all health facilities. The standard tool shall be administered; data collected and analysed; and a report generated. The key emerging issues from the routine client feedback initiative shall be discussed by the respective management to improve the quality of health services in the national health system.

    Clients should be informed and sensitized about the initiative by health workers during health education talks at all levels of interface with clients and through mass media and community engagement channels. At this point, clients should be informed that the time taken for the feedback is less than 10 minutes.

    3.1.2 How the routine client satisfaction feedback tool shall be implemented?¶
      1. The MoH standard routine client satisfaction feedback tool shall be used to collect feedback from clients about the quality of services provided in the health facility and community.
      1. The health facility management shall plan to routinely collect client feedback information on selected days of the month and analyze it monthly.
      1. A Quick Response (QR) code will be permanently displayed in all the departments for clients with smart phones to access and give feedback at anytime, and the findings will be included in the monthly report.
      1. The health facility management shall guide on the days to be considered for data collection, designate responsible persons and select the departments to be involved in the routine client feedback initiative.
      1. There should be a focal person appointed by the health facility management and all in charges of the sub units in the facility shall actively participate in the initiative and addressing the gaps identified from the routine client feedback.
      1. The data from routine client feedback should be discussed in the monthly health facility management meetings and actions taken to address client needs.
      1. Feedback should be given to staff during quarterly staff meetings by the health facility leadership.
      1. The health facility will identify and utilize different avenues to establish a feedback loop, including but not limited to the following:

    • • Established governance structures (HUMCs and Hospital Management Boards)

    • • Health talks
    • • Community dialogue meetings
    • • Community resource persons and platforms e.g., religious leaders, political leaders, community leaders and other opinion leaders
    • • Media (local radio, TV, print media, social media)
    • • Community outreaches, etc
    3.1.3 What do you need to inform the client of before obtaining their feedback?¶
    • • Explain their role as key stakeholders in improving the quality of health services in the health facility and community. They have a right to demand for better quality of services provided to them by the health facility.
    • • Assure the client that participation is voluntary; feedback is confidential; feedback will be analysed in aggregate; there are no reprisals from giving their opinions whatsoever and that client personal identifiers shall not be captured anywhere on the tool.
    3.1.4 How to administer the routine client satisfaction feedback tool:¶

      1. Clients can provide feedback by scanning a QR code displayed at a readily accessible location (e.g., notice board)
      1. Self-administered approach where the clients are provided with the routine client satisfaction tool to fill by themselves
      1. Interviewer administered tool where a designated person will interview the client

    Where the QR code is used, clients can submit feedback anytime while at the health facility or after leaving the facility.

    3.1.5 Who should be interviewed?¶
    • • Patients/clients or caretakers who have accessed services in the health facility. Effort should be made to cover different service units in the health facility.
    • • The tool shall be administered individually, in privacy and clients shall not be discriminated for services on the account of their views.
    3.1.6 Who should be excluded from giving feedback?¶

    Patients in critical condition, or unable to engage in conversation, and those who decline to share their experience of services.

    3.1.7 How many clients should be interviewed monthly for feedback?¶

    The following minimum number of clients/caretakers are recommended to be interviewed monthly per level of health facility. The number of clients interviewed should be fairly/randomly distributed across the days of the month.

    National & Regional Referral Hospitals General Hospitals Health Center IVs Health Centers IIIs Health Centre II Community eg outreach
    60 40 40 30 10 5

    NB. The minimum number of clients doesn’t include feedback collected via the QR code

    4.1 Data storage and access¶

    Routine client feedback data and reports will be uploaded into the Open Data Kit (ODK)Central, housed at the MoH-Division of Health Information (DHI). The different levels of stakeholders will be granted access rights by the responsible officer at the MoH-DHI.

    5.1 Using the findings¶
    5.1.1 National stakeholder utilization of data¶
      1. The MoH-SCAPP department will generate the overall client feedback from all regions. The data shall be discussed quarterly by the department, technical working group (Governance Standards and Policy Regulation), Senior Management Committee, and finally Health Policy Advisory Committee.
      1. The data will be used to guide MoH actions to address gaps during supportive supervision and other deliberate interventions to improve quality of care, e.g., Maternal Newborn Child Health Quality of Care, Local Maternity and Neonatal System, Maternal Perinatal Death Surveillance and Response, quality improvement (QI) platforms.
      1. Additionally, briefs will be included in the quarterly performance review meetings and reports from the Directorate of Governance and Regulation.
    5.1.2 Regional stakeholder utilization of data¶
      1. The RRH will consolidate routine client feedback from the RRH, the districts and urban authorities within the catchment area of the RRH.

    The regional routine client feedback data shall be disseminated to all key stakeholders and discussed routinely (monthly/quarterly) using regional platforms including but not limited to the following:

    • • Regional performance review meetings
    • • Regional QI Committee meeting (RECO)
    • • Regional Reproductive Health, Maternal and Child Health and Nutrition (RMNCAH-N)
    • • Local Maternity and Neonatal Systems (LMNS)

    • • Regional health assemblies

      1. The regional QI officer, biostatistician and facility QI focal person shall be the focal points for dashboard and actions taken for the previous client feedback initiatives.
      1. In addition, the regional QI officer and biostastician shall generate and distribute analytics inform of dashboards on client feedback parameters to the region.
    5.1.3 District level utilization of data¶

    The district will consolidate routine client feedback from the health facilities (including private) within their catchment area. The data from routine client feedback shall be discussed during the district quarterly meetings including:

    • • District performance review meeting
    • • District QI Committee meeting (DICO)
    • • Partner coordination meeting
    • • District support supervision of facilities

    The district biostatistician and district QI focal person shall generate analytics in form of dashboards and graphs on routine client satisfaction parameters.

    5.1.4 Health facility¶
      1. The health facility head shall consolidate routine client feedback from the sub units, develop action plans and give feedback to clients. The client feedback data shall be discussed during the routine monthly management and general staff meetings.
      1. Monthly analytics in the form of dashboards and charts/graphs shall be developed and shared within the facility teams.
      1. A brief update on the routine client feedback shall be shared with the governance structures (Health Unit Management Committees)/Hospital Management Boards.
    5.1.5 Community¶

    Community resource persons (village health teams, community health extension workers, etc) will collect client feedback information during community-based service delivery interventions such as outreaches. The data shall be submitted to the nearby facility for inclusion in the analysis. The findings shall be relayed back to the community teams for dissemination.

    5.1.6 Acting on the feedback at facility level¶
    • Step 1: Review the feedback using graphs, charts, trends and dashboard to identify common issues or patterns that need attention.
    • Step 2: Prioritise action items: Rank the identified issues based on their impact on the overall customer experience. Focus on the areas of client feedback that will have the most significant positive impact when improved.
    • Step 3: Conduct a root cause analysis using QI tools such as 5 ‘Whys’ to get to the bottom of the problem.
    • Step 4: The identified problem areas require focused interventions. The QI team, inclusive of relative stakeholders in the facility, recommend solutions.
    • Step 5: Develop a clear plan of action to address the identified issues. Assign responsibilities and set deadlines to ensure accountability using the template below. As much as possible, low-cost and context-based interventions should be used for short-term improvements.
    Gaps Interventions to address the gaps Timelines Responsible person
    • Step 6: Monitor implementation of intervention to help you assess the effectiveness of your actions and make any necessary adjustments.
    6.1 Data analysis, visualization¶
    • 6.1.1 Data analysis

    The routine client satisfaction feedback tool has been integrated in digital platforms to analyse routine client feedback in real-time and automatically convert it into graphs, charts, and trends that are easy to understand using MoH digital systems. The visualizations are designed to mirror strengths and gaps from the routine client feedback and inform on the areas for quality improvement efforts within healthcare facilities.

    • 6.1.2 Visualisation for client feedback will display

    • • Automated graphs, charts, and dashboards for each client feedback parameter and overall satisfaction rate layered from national, regional, district, facility and facility type.

    • • The thresholds of routine client satisfaction are as follows; Excellent and good are considered as satisfied while fair and poor are considered as unsatisfied.
    • • Pareto charts showing the dominant gaps that contribute to 80% of client dissatisfaction.

    Users will visualize the data in diverse formats by selecting dropdown options. These options enable the display of different scenarios, including routine client satisfaction parameters categorized by variables such as sex, age, service point, facility type, facility level, district, region, or national level or period. Users can also compare single variables against any selection option.

    7.1 Supportive supervision¶

    All supervision should prioritize and aim to address issues emerging from the routine client feedback.

    Appendix 1: Routine Client Satisfaction Feedback Tool¶

    This tool is used to obtain clients' feedback about their experience with the services and promote quality improvement, accountability and transparency within the healthcare system

    REGION DISTRICT
    HEALTH FACILITY Reporting period (MM/YYYY)
    Service unit 1= OPD; 2=Medical ward; 3=Maternity; 4=Antenatal; 5=EPI/YCC/ Postnatal; 6=Surgical ward; 7= ART, 8= Community Services; 9= Other, specify
    Age (Write age in complete years) Sex:
    1. Please rate each of the following according to your

    experience today on a scale of 1 to 4 where ‘1’ means Poor, ‘2’ Fair, ‘3’ Good and ‘4’ Excellent    		 4 Excellent 3 Good 2 Fair 1 Poor
    a. Overall cleanliness of the facility
    b. Overall timeliness of services
    c. Privacy
    d. Respect
    e. Medicines were available during this visit
    f. Received all the services you needed during this visit
    g. Health workers were available to provide the services you needed during this visit
    h. Accessed health/medical information about your condition and what needs to be done
    i. Cost of services (where services are formally paid for) Free Affordable Not Affordable
    2. Did you pay any charges that were not receipted in the form of a bribe? Yes No
    3. For the services you received, were you educated on them and your permission requested? Yes No
    4. Overall, were you satisfied with the services you received? Yes No
    5. Give one area of your choice where the facility should improve?
    Appendix 2: Action plan for the health facility¶
    Identified gaps Interventions to address the gaps Timelines Responsible person
    Appendix 3: Operational definition¶

    Overall cleanliness of the facility: Refers to the physical state of cleanliness and tidiness observed throughout the healthcare facility, including waiting areas, compound, restrooms, corridors, and other public spaces. It encompasses the absence of dirt, dust, clutter, and unpleasant odors. (Client’s feedback may be based on their visual observations and impressions).

    Overall timeliness of services: Refers to the client experience and observation of the time taken from arrival to delivery of services and encompasses the duration of waiting times for consultations, tests, treatment, and other healthcare interventions.

    Privacy: Refers to client perception of the extent to which their personal information, discussions, examinations, and treatments are kept confidential and protected from unauthorized access or disclosure within the facility. It encompasses physical privacy, such

    • as the availability of private consultation rooms and partitions to shield clients from view during examinations or discussions with healthcare providers. It also includes measures to keep confidential records, verbal communications, and any other sensitive information exchanged between clients and healthcare staff.

    Respect: Refers to the manner in which clients are treated with dignity, consideration, and courtesy by healthcare providers and staff throughout their interactions within the facility. It encompasses behavior and communication that demonstrate recognition of clients' autonomy, cultural beliefs, values, and preferences, as well as sensitivity to individual needs and concerns. Respectful conduct includes active listening, empathy, non-judgmental attitudes, and provision of understandable information about diagnoses, treatments, and care plans.

    Medicines were available during this visit: Refers to the accessibility and availability of prescribed medications or necessary pharmaceutical products within the healthcare facility

    • at the time of the client's visit. It encompasses medical supplies required for treatment or management of the client’s health conditions.

    Received all the services you needed during this visit: Refers to whether clients' healthcare needs were fully addressed and met during their visit to the healthcare facility. It encompasses the extent to which clients received all the necessary medical examinations, treatments, diagnostic tests, consultations, and other healthcare services required to address their health concerns or conditions. The assessment considers whether clients felt that their healthcare needs were comprehensively evaluated, understood, and attended to by healthcare providers and staff during the visit.

    Health workers were available to provide the services you needed during this visit: Refers to the availability and accessibility of healthcare professionals within the facility to deliver the required services to clients during their visit. It encompasses the presence of qualified and skilled healthcare providers, including doctors, nurses, specialists, and other allied health professionals, who are capable of addressing clients' healthcare needs effectively. The assessment considers whether clients encountered any difficulties or delays in accessing healthcare services due to the availability or absence of healthcare workers during their visit.

    Accessed health/medical information about your condition and what needs to be done: Refers to whether clients were provided with comprehensive information regarding their health condition and the necessary steps to be taken for treatment or management during their visit to the healthcare facility. It encompasses the extent to which clients received clear explanations, instructions, and educational materials from healthcare providers regarding their diagnosis, prognosis, treatment options, medication regimen,

    lifestyle modifications, and follow-up care. The assessment considers whether clients felt adequately informed and empowered to make informed decisions about their health and healthcare interventions based on the information provided during the visit.

    Able to access health services of your interest in your local community evaluates the ease and availability of healthcare services within the local community that align with clients' preferences and needs. It encompasses the extent to which clients can readily locate and access a variety of healthcare services, including primary care, preventive services, and additional services, such as immunization, health education, diagnostic tests and community outreach services within their locality.

    Cost of services (where services are formally paid for): Evaluates the financial implications associated with accessing healthcare services. It encompasses the expenses incurred by clients for receiving healthcare services, including consultation fees, treatment costs, diagnostic tests, medications, medical procedures, and any other out-of-pocket expenses or co-payments required for accessing services. The assessment considers the affordability and transparency of service costs, as well as any financial assistance or insurance coverage available to mitigate the financial burden on clients. It also includes clients' perceptions of the value received in relation to the cost paid for healthcare services.

    For the services you received, were you educated on them and your permission requested? Assesses the extent to which clients received information about the healthcare services provided to them and whether their consent was sought before proceeding with the services. It encompasses the provision of clear explanations, instructions, and educational materials by healthcare providers regarding the nature, purpose, risks, benefits, and alternatives of the services offered. Additionally, it evaluates whether clients were given the opportunity to ask questions, express concerns, and make informed decisions about their healthcare based on the information provided. The assessment considers whether healthcare providers obtained clients' consent before initiating any procedures, treatments, or interventions, respecting clients' autonomy and rights to make decisions about their own care.

    Did you pay any charges that were not receipted in the form of a bribe? Assesses whether clients made any unofficial payments or financial transactions not documented through receipts, often referred to as bribes, in exchange for healthcare services or preferential treatment within the health facility. It encompasses any monetary exchanges or gifts offered by clients to healthcare providers or staff outside of official billing procedures and documented financial transactions. The assessment aims to identify instances of corruption, unethical conduct, or abuse of power within the healthcare system, where clients may feel compelled to pay additional fees or gratuities to expedite or ensure access to healthcare services.

    Overall, were you satisfied with the services you received? Overall, were you satisfied with the services you received? evaluates clients' overall satisfaction with the healthcare services they received during their visit to the facility. It encompasses overall clients' perceptions, feelings, and judgments regarding the healthcare services provided, as well as their overall experience and outcomes of care. The assessment considers various factors that contribute to clients' satisfaction, including the accessibility, responsiveness, competence, empathy, and respectfulness of healthcare providers and staff, timeliness, as well as the availability and effectiveness of healthcare facilities and treatments.
